coredata.dc:title: Red emissive carbon dots-based polymer beads for recyclable and ultra-sensitive detection of malachite green in fish tissue
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Shuo
Li, Jinquan
Sun, Pengjuan
Tao, Jian
Fu, Yanfeng
Yang, Ran
Li, Zhaohui
Qu, Lingbo
coredata.dc:description: As a widely used antiparasitic agent, malachite green (MG) is a severe threat to human health with strong genotoxicity and carcinogenicity. So, simple, rapid and accurate determination of MG is significant for the food safety. This study prepared red emissive carbon dots (CDs) as a novel fluorescence sensing platform for MG detection based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The rapid sensing could be completed within 1 s with a low detection limit of 12.9 nmol/L. The fluorescence of CDs quenched by MG could be successfully in situ regenerated by further adding trace HSO3 - and recycled at least ten times. To realize simple detection on site, the recyclable, simple and portable detection device were fabricated by loading CDs into the polymer beads. Coupled with mobile photography and RGB (Red, Green, Blue) analysis, both the accurate quantitative analysis and semi-quantitative visual analysis for the real fish samples could be achieved within 5 min. Such a simple detection mode without any instrument provides an efficient strategy for the development of fast sensing and smart detection devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Malachite green
Inner filter effect
Recyclable detection
Detection beads

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of detection beads and samples detection
Characterizations and morphology of the R-CDs
The detection of MG by polymer beads

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Zhongyuan Scholars program ( 202101510005 ), the Science and Technology Project of Market Supervision Administration of Henan Province ( 2022sj30 , 2022sj22 ), the 111 Project ( D20003 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U1804136 , 21974125 ), and the Collaborative Innovation Project of Zhengzhou (Zhengzhou University) (Grant no. 18XTZX12002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of the R-CDs. Inset: image of size distribution. (B) FT-IR spectrum of the R-CDs. (C−F) XPS of the R-CDs.
(A) The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs in PBS under 530 nm excitation. (B) The fluorescence spectra of R-CDs under different excitation. (C) The photostability of R-CDs for 3600 s.

(A) Absorption spectrum of MG and fluorescence spectrum of the R-CDs (λex = 530 nm). Insets: the fluorescence spectra of MG and the R-CDs before and after MG addition, and the photos of the R-CDs with or without MG and MG under the fluorescent field. (B) Fluorescence versus time of the R-CDs (15 μg/mL, λex = 530 nm) in the presence of 15 μM MG. (C) The fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs with or without MG. (D) The fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs (15 μg/mL, λex = 530 nm) with different concentrations (0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μM) of MG. (E) The linear calibration plots for MG detection. The linear range is 1.0–25 μM. (F) The value of ΔF/F0 of the R-CDs under other interfering materials in the absence or presence of MG. The concentrations of all tested substances and MG were 15 µM. Ex = 530 nm. Inset: the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs under other interfering materials in the absence or presence of MG. Ex = 530 nm.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the R-CDs (15 μg/mL, λex = 530 nm) and 15 μM MG in different concentrations of HSO3 - (0–250 μM). Inset: the plot of fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs (15 μg/mL) and MG (15 μM) with increasing HSO3 - concentrations from 0 to 250 μM at 595 nm. (B) Fluorescence versus time of the R-CDs (15 μg/mL, λex = 530 nm) in the presence of 15 μM MG and 100 μM HSO3 -, respectively. (C) Fluorescence intensity of R-CDs (15 μg/mL, λex = 530 nm) after repeated treatment with MG and HSO3 - for ten times.

(A) Photographs of polymer beads doped R-CDs in the absence and presence of MG and HSO3 - under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) Photographs of the color change of the R-CDs-doped polymer beads with different MG concentrations (0–0.5 μM) under a 365 nm UV lamp. (C) The linear relationship between the R values and MG concentrations (0.1–0.5 μM).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of R-CDs and a promising fluorescence sensing strategy of MG detection.
Determination results for MG in fish tissue samples via fluorescence method (n = 3).
Determination results of MG in fish tissue via the R-CDs-doped polymer beads and RGB analysis technology (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Red emissive carbon dots-based polymer beads for recyclable and ultra-sensitive detection of malachite green in fish tissue

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a widely used antiparasitic agent, malachite green (MG) is a severe threat to human health with strong genotoxicity and carcinogenicity. So, simple, rapid and accurate determination of MG is significant for the food safety. This study prepared red emissive carbon dots (CDs) as a novel fluorescence sensing platform for MG detection based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The rapid sensing could be completed within 1 s with a low detection limit of 12.9 nmol/L. The fluorescence of CDs quenched by MG could be successfully in situ regenerated by further adding trace HSO3 - and recycled at least ten times. To realize simple detection on site, the recyclable, simple and portable detection device were fabricated by loading CDs into the polymer beads. Coupled with mobile photography and RGB (Red, Green, Blue) analysis, both the accurate quantitative analysis and semi-quantitative visual analysis for the real fish samples could be achieved within 5 min. Such a simple detection mode without any instrument provides an efficient strategy for the development of fast sensing and smart detection devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG), an N-methylated diaminotriphenylmethane drug, is widely used in disinfecting aquatic products because of its low cost and germicidal efficacy [1]. However, MG has potential carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic effects and could cause teratogenesis, cancer, and severe damage to our body [2]. It is clearly stipulated by many countries that malachite green residues shall not be detected in aquatic products [3]. In this case, the detection of MG is indeed of great importance for food security and individual health.

Up to now, several methods have been utilized for MG determination, such as electrochemical analysis [4,5], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6,7], liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (LC-MS) [8], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [9], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [10,11], immuno-chromatography (IC) [12,13], electrochemiluminescence method (ECL) [14] and fluorescence method [15,16]. Among these methods, the fluorescence method is becoming more and more attractive due to its simple operation, high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness and rapidity. Many fluorescent materials have been reported to be effective in detecting, such as CdTe quantum dots [17,18], lanthanide-chelated metal-organic frameworks [19,20], fluorescent dyes [21,22] and so on. However, there are still requirements for improvement in the sensitivity, selectivity and rapidity of detection to meet the actual detection needs. Song group [23] established the detection system of poly (ethylenimine) carbon dots (BPEI-CDs)-MnO2 nanosheets-butyrylcholinesterase (BCHE) for the determination of MG based on its enzyme inhibition of BChE activity. For this report, the fabrication of the detection system is very complex and cumbersome. Furthermore, appropriate temperature and long reaction time are needed for enzyme catalysis and enzyme inhibition leading to almost an hour time for samples detection. Li group [24] established N, S graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs)-CdTe QDs system for MG detection due to the non-covalent interaction of MG with N,S-GQDs and CdTe QDs. This non-covalent interaction was easily influenced by other external factors and the selectivity is limited for the real samples detection. Designing novel fluorescent sensors and fabricating new detection system are imperative to solve or improve the above problems in the detection of MG.

Inner filter effect (IFE) is a simple strategy to fabricate fluorescent sensors, which is based on the excitation or emission of the fluorescent probe absorbed by the analyte [25,26]. Carbon dots (CDs), a unique fluorescent nanomaterial, have superior physical and chemical properties consisting of high resistance to photobleaching, excellent water-soluble ability, and low toxicity [27]. Because MG has a typical absorption peak at 618 nm, which has the long wavelength absorption effect to avoid the interference of the matrix, relative to most analytes, IFE is more suitable for obtaining highly sensitive and selective detection of MG [28,29]. Therefore, in this study, novel CDs with red emission (R-CDs) at 595 nm were rationally designed and prepared via microwave method using julolidine, 8-hydroxyjulolidine, and citrate as carbon sources ( Scheme 1). The fluorescence of R-CDs could be greatly quenched in the presence of MG through IFE and further restored in situ after the addition of HSO3 -, thus making a rapid and recyclable detection of MG. To meet the needs of on-site real-time detection, the R-CDs was immobilized into the polymer beads and formed fluorescence sensing beads. Coupled with mobile photography and RGB (Red, Green, Blue) analysis, both the accurate quantitative analysis and semi-quantitative visual analysis for the real fish samples could be achieved within 5 min through these sensing beads. The results obtained in the work make an economical, practical, and straightforward way for in site MG detection in aquatic products or environment. Such a simple detection mode without any instrument provides an efficient strategy for the development of fast sensing and smart detection devices.

Julolidine (69.3 mg), 8-hydroxyjulolidine (75.7 mg), and citrate (153.6 mg) were dissolved to 16 mL absolute ethanol. Then, the solution was heated at 160 ℃ for 2 h via microwave method to acquire a dark brown solution, and column chromatography was used to purify with dichloromethane: ethanol (10:1, v-v) mixture as eluent. Finally, the obtained solution was evaporated to afford R-CDs as a purple solid, followed by dissolving in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to be a stock solution (10 mg/mL) and storing at room temperature.

Various concentrations of MG (0–25 µM) were injected to the phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH = 7.4) containing the R-CDs (15 μg/mL). Then, the intensity of fluorescence at 595 nm was recorded, and the fluorescence quenching degree (ΔF/F0, ΔF = F0 − F, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs without MG, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs with MG) was calculated.

The recyclability of the proposed method was investigated by adding a variety of concentrations of NaHSO3 (0–250 μM) into the reaction system of R-CDs-MG containing the R-CDs (15 μg/mL) and the MG (15 μM). The degree of fluorescence recovery of the R-CDs-MG was calculated according to the fluorescence values of the system after adding NaHSO3.

Two kinds of fish (perch and crucian) were bought from the located supermarket as real samples. The treatment method for fish tissue was mainly according to the literature method [30]. 1 g fish tissue of back was weighed accurately, mashed completely and dissolved into 5 mL acetonitrile. After ultrasound and centrifugation (12000 rpm) for 5 min, the obtained supernatant was the sample solution for detection.

When detect MG in fish samples, different MG concentrations (0, 5, 10, and 15 μM) were injected in the solution containing R-CDs (15 μg/mL) and supernatant of samples. Then, the intensity of fluorescence at 595 nm was recorded. Recovery and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of detection beads and samples detection
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty polymer beads were immersed in the R-CDs (15 μg/mL) aqueous solution for 30 min and they were dried at room temperature for 6 h. The dried beads were the detection beads and named the R-CDs-doped polymer beads. When detection, the R-CDs-doped polymer beads were immersed into different concentrations of MG solutions for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs-doped polymer beads were observed and photographed under a 365 UV light source. The detailed steps of MG determination in real samples were described in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and morphology of the R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the R-CDs was explored by transmission electron microscopic (TEM) ( Fig. 1A). The R-CDs is spherical and its Gaussian particle size distribution ranges from 1.5 to 3.5 nm with an average size of 2.5 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy showed the characteristic band at 3439 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibrations of O−H. The bands at 2925 and 2856 cm−1 were the asymmetrical stretching vibrations and the symmetrical stretching vibrations of C−H, respectively. The band at 1735 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibrations of CO and 1630 cm−1 explained benzene skeleton vibration of CO. Other peaks at 1180, 1081, and 474 cm−1 can be clarified to the stretching vibrations of C−N, C−O, and C−H [31,32]. The elemental species and content of the R-CDs was studied through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). From Fig. 1C, peaks corresponding to C 1 s (284.8 eV), N 1 s (399.8 eV), and O 1 s (537.8 eV) were acquired and demonstrated relative content of C, N, and O of the non-hydrogen atoms of the R-CDs, 85.28%, 8.30%, and 6.42%, respectively. The forms of C, N, and O in the R-CDs were investigated by further analyzing high-resolution XPS spectra of the R-CDs. According to Fig. 1D, the analysis illustrated three species of carbon, called C−C/CC, C−O/C−N, and CO corresponding to 284.8 eV, 285.8 eV, and 287.3 eV, respectively. Three of nitrogen were pyridine nitrogen at 398.3 eV, amino nitrogen at 399.3 eV and pyrrole nitrogen at 400.6 eV in Fig. 1E. Two of O1 s were CO at 531.3 eV and C−O/C−O−C at 533.1 eV (Fig. 1F). The above data demonstrated the R-CDs have been successfully synthesized and rich functional groups of carboxyl and hydroxyl on the surface of the R-CDs.

The optical characteristics of the R-CDs were characterized by absorption and emission spectra. As depicted in Fig. 2A, the maximum absorption and emission wavelengths of the R-CDs were 570 nm and 595 nm, respectively. With the increasing excitation wavelength from 500 to 560 nm, the position of the maximum emission peak at 595 nm was unchanged, indicating that the emission of the R-CDs is not dependent of excitation wavelength (Fig. 2B). And the R-CDs were proven to have excellent photostability under continuous fluorescence light excitation for 3600 s (Fig. 2C). Also, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs remained almost constant in PBS buffers at various pH values (5.0–9.0) (Fig. S1). In addition, in Fig. S2, the average fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs is 3.99 ns. All these lays a foundation for the construction of stable and excellent R-CDs.

As seen in Fig. 3A, the emission spectrum of the R-CDs overlaps largely with the absorption spectrum of MG, so there is a higher IFE between them. And when MG was introduced, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreased greatly within 1 s (Fig. 3B), illustrating that MG could be detected by R-CDs instantly. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime analysis has been proposed to interpret the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the R-CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs is no obvious variation after addition of MG (Fig. 3C), verifying again that IFE is the major mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of R-CDs originated from MG [33,34].

To achieve the best effect on detection, the pH value of the PBS buffer for the determination of MG was investigated. From Fig. S3, the fluorescence quenching degree upon adding MG (15 μM) were almost unchanged in the pH range of 5.0–9.0. Considering physiological environments, the pH of 7.4 was selected as the optimum pH condition. Under pH 7.4, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreased gradually upon adding various MG concentrations from 0 to 25 μM (Fig. 3D). In addition, in the range of 1.0–25 μM, there is a wider linear range and the linear equation was ΔF/F0 = 0.0205 [MG] + 0.2306 (R2 = 0.996) (Fig. 3E). And the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 12.9 nM with the formula 3σ (σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity of 10 blank samples).

Subsequently, the selectivity and anti-interference performance of the R-CDs were estimated. As seen in Fig. 3F, metal ions (Na+, K+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ca2+, and Fe2+), amino acids (Glu and Cys), synthetic toxic organic dyes (carmine, MBT, and Sudan Ⅲ), and organic molecules (tetracycline, enrofloxacin, and norfloxacin) have almost no effect on MG detection, illustrating the high specificity and anti-interference ability of the R-CDs toward MG.

HSO3 -, a very active nucleophile, is prone to take place Michael addition reaction and MG is the electron acceptor of Michael addition reaction [35,36]. HSO3 - could react with MG in 50 s and form a new compound with a very weak absorption at 618 nm upon adding 100 μM HSO3 - (Fig. S4), leading to the fluorescence recovery of R-CDs-MG system. The optimum reaction ratio of MG and HSO3 - was investigated by adding different concentrations of HSO3 - (0–250 μM) to monitor the degree of fluorescence recovery of the R-CDs (15 μg/mL)-MG (15 μM) system ( Fig. 4A). It was found that when the ratio of MG: HSO3 - was 7:1, the fluorescence intensity returned to the maximum and trended to be stable. At optimal ratio of MG and HSO3 -, the fluorescence could be recovered to the maximum within 50 s (Fig. 4B). When MG was added again into the system containing 15 μg/mL R-CDs, 15 μM MG, and 100 μM HSO3 -, the fluorescence quenching is almost the same as the previous time, suggesting that the recyclable detection could be successfully achieved in site without any purification. After 10 cycles of use, the R-CDs still maintain 95% detection capacity, indicating the recyclability of the detection method (Fig. 4C).

Relative to the reported fluorescence sensors (Table S1), the proposed R-CDs sensor exhibits excellent advantages of greater reproducibility, faster response, higher sensitivity and better selectivity, and is worthy of further use in practical MG determination.

As described in section, the concentration of MG in crucian and perch samples were detected. There is hardly any fluorescence quenching, suggesting that there is no MG. Then, MG standard solutions were added to the extractions of crucian and perch to make real samples with low, medium, and high concentrations of MG (5, 10, and 15 μM) for recovery experiments. As shown in Table 1, the recovery efficiency ranged from 94.8% to 103.0%, with RSDs less than 3.7%, verifying that the R-CDs could accurately and sensitively detect MG in fish tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of MG by polymer beads
ce:section.ce:para: It is far from meeting the actual detection requirements for just owning to the laboratory method. The determination of MG in fish tissue belongs to the field of food safety market supervision, which requires the detection of MG to be simple, rapid, economical and suitable for on-site inspection. For more practical application, the R-CDs were immobilized into the polymer beads just through adsorption method. As seen in Fig. 5A, the polymer beads treated with R-CDs solution displayed bright red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. Coupled with mobile photography and RGB analysis technology, the RGB values of five R-CDs-doped polymer detection beads in the same batch and in five batches were calculated (Fig. S5). The RSDs of the RGB of different detection beads in the same batch and five different batches are no more than 5%, proving the reproducibility and stability of the fabrication of R-CDs-doped polymer detection beads (Table S2).

When detection, it just needs to put the R-CDs-doped polymer beads into the test solution. As shown in Fig. 5B, after putting the R-CDs-doped polymer beads into MG solutions with different concentrations (0.1–0.5 μM) for 5 min, the color of the R-CDs-doped polymer beads faded gradually as increasing concentrations of MG. When the R-CDs-doped polymer beads were immersed in 0.4 μM MG solution, the color of R-CDs-doped polymer beads changed from red to almost colorless. With this obvious color change, 0.4 μM MG in the solution could be rapidly identified based on semi-quantitative analysis. And the values of R channel exhibit an acceptable linear relationship (Fig. 5C) with the increasing MG concentration in the range of 0.1–0.5 μM (R2 = 0.995). Exception of the above excellent performance, what's more surprising is that the detection beads have strong enrichment for MG, and the adsorption capacity is 8.5 μg per R-CDs-doped polymer bead (Fig. S6). Moreover, the color of R-CDs-doped polymer beads changed from colorless to red fluorescence after placing them into HSO3 - solution, demonstrating that reusability of the R-CDs-doped polymer beads (Fig. 5A).

To investigate the practical application feasibility of the R-CDs-doped polymer beads, the R-CDs-doped polymer beads were put into the extraction of the fish samples without or with MG solutions (0.15, 0.25, and 0.35 μM). As shown in Table 2, using photographic imaging and RGB analysis, the recovery rates ranged from 98.3% to 107.3%, with the RSDs less than 7.1%, indicating that the R-CDs-doped polymer beads could also be exploited for accurate detection of MG in fish tissue. Relative to the detection with fluorescent equipment, such detection beads without any equipment are more meet the need of rapid screening of market regulation.

In conclusion, red emissive CDs were synthesized and used as MG probes based on IFE. The fluorescence of the R-CDs quenched by MG could be recovered in situ after adding HSO3 - due to the Michael addition reaction between them, thus, a recyclable detection could be achieved. The R-CDs could detect MG instantaneously, sensitively and selectively with a lower detection limit of 12.9 nM. To make a more convenient and immediate practical detection, the R-CDs-based detection beads were fabricated. The detection beads could enrich MG to realize the purpose of sensitive detection of MG. Coupled with mobile photography and RGB color analysis technology, the R-CDs-doped polymer beads were successfully used for the accurate quantitative analysis of MG in fish samples. Simple R-CDs-based detection beads mode without any instrument could be carried out directly to make instant detection or in situ monitoring, and provides an innovative strategy for the design of the smart and simple fluorescent sensors.

Shuo Cai: Data curation, conceptualization, visualization, methodology, formal analysis, writing - original draft. Jinquan Li: Data curation, conceptualization. Pengjuan Sun: Data curation, conceptualization. Jian Tao: Funding acquisition. Yanfeng Fu: Funding acquisition. Ran Yang: Writing - review & editing, supervision, funding acquisition. Zhaohui Li: Writing - review & editing, funding acquisition. Lingbo Qu: Writing - review & editing, funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Zhongyuan Scholars program (202101510005), the Science and Technology Project of Market Supervision Administration of Henan Province (2022sj30, 2022sj22), the 111 Project (D20003), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1804136, 21974125), and the Collaborative Innovation Project of Zhengzhou (Zhengzhou University) (Grant no. 18XTZX12002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133311.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Uptake, tissue distribution, and metabolism of malachite green in the channel catfish (Ictalurus punctatus)
Can. J. Fish. Aquat. Sci.

J. Hazard. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of malachite green in fish tissue using red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped carbon dots
Food Chem.

Anal. Chim. Acta

J. Agric. Food Chem.

Anal. Methods

Methods. 7 (2015) 8138–8145. https://doi.org/10.1039/C5AY01611H.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new aptamer/black phosphorous interdigital electrode for malachite green detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shuo Cai is currently a MS candidate in analytical chemistry under the supervision of Professor Lingbo Qu. Her current research interests focus on fluorescent carbon dots and their applications in food detection.
Jinquan Li is a master, who graduated from College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Zhengzhou University. His research interests focused on carbon dots and their biological applications.

Pengjuan Sun is a master, who graduated from College of Chemistry, Green Catalysis Center, Zhengzhou University. Her research interests focused on fluorescent probes and their biological applications.

Jian Tao is a senior engineer of Key Laboratory of Food Safety Quick Testing and Smart Supervision Technology for State Market Regulation, Zhengzhou. His current research interests focus on the detection of food safety.

Yanfeng Fu is a senior engineer of Key Laboratory of Food Safety Quick Testing and Smart Supervision Technology for State Market Regulation and Zhengzhou Zhongdao Biotechnology company limited. His current research interests focus on the detection of food safety.
Ran Yang is a professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. Her research interests are focused on fluorescent sensors and their applications in food detection.
Zhaohui Li is a professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His main research interests focus on fluorescent probes and their applications in rapid detection.
Lingbo Qu is a professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His research interests are focused on chemical and biological sensors.
